module.label: Pediatric pallor, anemia, bruising, bleeding, or petechiae

use_when: Child or adolescent with pallor, suspected anemia, fatigue with anemia concern, easy bruising, petechiae, purpura, epistaxis, gum bleeding, heavy menstrual bleeding, GI bleeding, hematuria, abnormal CBC, thrombocytopenia, or concern for ITP, iron deficiency, hemolysis, leukemia, lymphoma, or transfusion need.

Pediatric hematology workup needs age-specific safety data, bleeding burden, iron and nutrition risk, hemolysis features, malignancy red flags, individualized bedside maneuvers, CBC and smear interpretation, coagulopathy testing, transfusion thresholds, hematology consultation, and explicit limits for unstable bleeding.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use the adult bleeding, anemia, or GI-bleed pathway.

Child has airway compromise, shock, massive hemorrhage, severe altered mental status, suspected sepsis, or intracranial bleeding concern; activate local emergency, transfusion, critical care, or hematology pathway while documenting focused findings.

Primary rash, fever/sepsis, abdominal, urinary, neurologic, trauma, or safeguarding concern dominates; use that pediatric pathway and add this hematology module only when blood count, bruising, petechiae, pallor, bleeding, or transfusion decisions change management.

Known oncology, hemoglobinopathy, congenital bleeding disorder, bone marrow failure, transplant, or chronic transfusion protocol where local hematology guidance overrides this general clinician-support module.

modifier_add_on_policy.rationale: This validated pediatric hematology pack already screens fever, infection recurrence, petechiae with unwell appearance, and malignancy/lymphoma features; generic infection prompts should not replace source-backed heme routing.

differentialBuckets.label: Pediatric anemia or pallor: classify severity, symptom burden, production failure, blood loss, hemolysis, iron deficiency, B12 or folate deficiency, chronic disease, hemoglobinopathy, or malignancy pattern
differentialBuckets.action: Use symptoms, growth/nutrition, bleeding history, reticulocyte count, film, indices, ferritin, hemolysis tests, and red flags to separate common iron deficiency from urgent marrow, hemolysis, or bleeding causes.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH anemia guidance emphasizes red flag features, CBC/film pattern, reticulocytes, ferritin, hemolysis markers, and additional tests selected by clinical features.

source.source_section: assessment, investigations, red flags, and causes of anaemia
source.implementation_notes: Do not treat pallor or fatigue as benign until red flags, CBC/film, and severity context are reviewed.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Iron deficiency anemia, acute blood loss, hemolysis, marrow failure, B12 or folate deficiency, chronic disease, hemoglobinopathy, leukemia, or lymphoma.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes urgency, CBC/film and reticulocyte interpretation, ferritin or hemolysis testing, transfusion discussion, iron therapy, hematology referral, admission, and follow-up interval.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not available for this broad anemia frame; source-backed CBC, film, reticulocyte, ferritin, hemolysis, and red-flag findings drive management.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Normal appearance can coexist with chronic anemia; acute bleeding or hemolysis can deteriorate before repeat labs return.

differentialBuckets.label: Pediatric bruising, petechiae, purpura, thrombocytopenia, or ITP pattern
differentialBuckets.action: Separate a well child with isolated thrombocytopenia and otherwise normal full blood examination and film from sepsis, leukemia, coagulopathy, trauma, non-accidental injury, drug effect, or systemic disease.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH ITP defines ITP as isolated platelet count below 100 x 10^9/L in a well child with otherwise normal FBE and film; treatment is based on clinical features rather than platelet count alone.

source.source_section: key points, assessment, investigations, differential diagnosis, and management
source.implementation_notes: ITP is a diagnosis of exclusion; abnormal film, systemic symptoms, organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, or unwell appearance should route away from low-risk ITP.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Immune thrombocytopenia, leukemia, sepsis, DIC, coagulation disorder, vasculitis, trauma, non-accidental injury, or medication-associated thrombocytopenia.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes bleeding severity grading, activity precautions, hematology consultation, platelet or immunomodulatory treatment consideration, admission, and urgent alternate-diagnosis evaluation.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not available for the broad ITP frame; diagnosis requires source-defined exclusion of alternative causes.
differentialBuckets.limitations: A low platelet count alone does not determine treatment, and a child who is unwell is not a low-risk ITP presentation.

differentialBuckets.label: Possible leukemia or lymphoma: unexplained petechiae, bruising, pallor, persistent fatigue, fever, infections, bone pain, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, or weight loss
differentialBuckets.action: Treat unexplained petechiae as immediate specialist assessment and unexplained bruising, pallor, persistent fatigue, fever, infection, bone pain, or generalized lymphadenopathy as very urgent CBC and referral-routing cues.
differentialBuckets.rationale: NICE NG12 lists child and young-person red flags for leukemia and lymphoma, including unexplained petechiae, bruising, pallor, fatigue, fever, infection, bone pain, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, night sweats, pruritus, and weight loss.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs in children and young people
source.implementation_notes: NICE referral actions differ for children and young people and should not be replaced by adult cancer-pathway assumptions.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Leukemia, lymphoma, bone sarcoma mimic, marrow failure, infection, or inflammatory disease.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Positive features change urgency of CBC, specialist assessment, hematology/oncology referral, imaging when indicated, and discharge safety.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not available for this red-flag referral frame; NICE uses symptom combinations and urgency thresholds rather than LR+/LR-.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Many features are nonspecific, but unexplained combinations in children should not be dismissed because a single symptom is common.

differentialBuckets.label: Pediatric treatment frame: transfusion threshold, iron replacement, bleeding control, and specialty handoff depend on symptoms, hemoglobin, cause, and chronicity
differentialBuckets.action: Use Hb threshold, symptoms, bleeding/hemolysis, chronic disease, hemoglobinopathy, iron deficiency, and local blood-product procedures to decide transfusion, iron therapy, consultation, and follow-up.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH blood product guidance often indicates RBC transfusion below 70 g/L, may indicate transfusion at 70 to 90 g/L depending on clinical setting and anemia symptoms, and often avoids transfusion above 90 g/L; RCH iron deficiency guidance treats most children with oral iron and diet change.

source.source_section: indications for red blood cell transfusion
source.implementation_notes: Use local transfusion prescription and consent procedures; this module does not calculate product dose.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Transfusion need, iron deficiency treatment, specialty handoff, and follow-up safety.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Positive thresholds or symptoms change admission, IV access, group and screen, transfusion discussion, iron dose selection, and repeat lab timing.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to transfusion and iron-treatment thresholds; management follows source-defined thresholds and symptoms.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Transfusion thresholds vary with chronic anemia, hemoglobinopathy, cardiac disease, active bleeding, hemolysis, and local policy.

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.rationale: Fever with petechiae, purpura, bruising, pallor, infection recurrence, lymphadenopathy, or splenomegaly changes sepsis, leukemia, lymphoma, and admission urgency.
safetyChecks.management_change: Fever changes source-directed infection testing, sepsis pathway, hematology referral urgency, and discharge safety.
safetyChecks.options: ___ C/F / febrile / afebrile / hypothermic / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Antipyretics, timing, immunosuppression, or severe illness can mask fever.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data.

source.source_section: assessment and serious bacterial infection risk
source.implementation_notes: Keep temperature in baseline data, not physical exam.

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate and blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Measure heart rate and blood pressure with age-appropriate cuff and pediatric interpretation.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachycardia, hypotension, postural symptoms, hypertension, or shock changes bleeding, severe anemia, sepsis, and transfusion urgency.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal rate or BP changes resuscitation, IV access, type and screen, transfusion discussion, observation interval, admission, and escalation.
safetyChecks.options: HR ___ bpm / BP ___/___ mmHg / age-appropriate / tachycardic / bradycardic / hypotensive / hypertensive / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Pain, fever, anxiety, dehydration, cuff size, chronic anemia, and active bleeding affect interpretation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to vital signs as baseline clinical data.

source.source_section: red flags and monitoring
source.implementation_notes: Tachycardia can indicate severe anemia or ongoing blood loss.

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate and oxygen saturation
safetyChecks.action: Measure respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, and current oxygen delivery.
safetyChecks.rationale: Dyspnea, tachypnea, hypoxemia, or shortness of breath with lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly changes anemia severity, cardiopulmonary strain, lymphoma concern, and admission urgency.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal respiratory data changes oxygen support, transfusion urgency, chest imaging consideration, specialty referral, and monitoring level.
safetyChecks.options: RR ___/min / SpO2 ___% room air or oxygen / age-appropriate / tachypnea / hypoxemia / respiratory distress / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Crying, fever, pain, asthma, infection, and severe anemia affect respiratory rate and oxygen demand.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate or oxygen saturation as baseline clinical data.

source.source_section: shortness of breath with lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly in children
source.implementation_notes: Shortness of breath with lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly changes lymphoma referral urgency.

safetyChecks.label: Measure current weight
safetyChecks.action: Document current measured or best estimated weight in kg before medication, iron, blood-product, or emergency dosing decisions.
safetyChecks.rationale: Pediatric iron therapy, transfusion volume, emergency medicine, and product prescription depend on weight.
safetyChecks.management_change: Missing weight blocks reliable iron dosing, transfusion volume planning, and weight-based emergency management.
safetyChecks.options: Measured ___ kg / estimated ___ kg / recent reliable weight ___ kg / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Critical illness or immobility may require estimated weight and later confirmation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to weight as baseline clinical data.

source.source_section: oral iron supplementation dosing
source.implementation_notes: Weight belongs in baseline data, not physical exam maneuvers.

safetyChecks.label: Document active bleeding burden and mental status
safetyChecks.action: Document current bleeding site, estimated burden, recurrent episodes, hemodynamic symptoms, and AVPU or age-appropriate mental status.
safetyChecks.rationale: Active bleeding, syncope, altered state, or heavy ongoing losses change immediate stabilization, transfusion, reversal, and specialty escalation.
safetyChecks.management_change: Active heavy bleeding or altered state changes IV access, labs, type and screen, transfusion, resuscitation, observation, and admission.
safetyChecks.options: No active bleeding / mild mucocutaneous bleeding / heavy or recurrent bleeding / suspected internal bleeding / AVPU alert / altered / unable to assess
safetyChecks.limitations: Bleeding volume estimates can be inaccurate; caregiver report, pads/tissues, stool or emesis description, and serial vitals matter.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to active bleeding burden or mental status as baseline safety data.

source.source_section: clinical features and treatment based on bleeding features
source.implementation_notes: Treatment decisions in ITP and bleeding presentations depend on clinical bleeding features, not platelet count alone.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask age growth and baseline hematology context
requiredQuestions.text: What is the child's age, growth trajectory, baseline energy, recent weight change, prematurity or chronic disease history, prior CBC pattern, and any known hemoglobinopathy, bleeding disorder, transfusion, oncology, transplant, or hematology follow-up?
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: infant / child / adolescent / poor growth or weight loss / chronic disease / known hemoglobinopathy / known bleeding disorder / prior transfusion or hematology care / none known / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask current age and whether symptoms differ from baseline.
Ask about growth faltering, weight loss, delayed development, or chronic disease.
Ask about known hemoglobinopathy, bleeding disorder, marrow condition, transfusion history, oncology care, transplant, or hematology plan.
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for every pediatric pallor, anemia, bleeding, bruising, petechiae, thrombocytopenia, or abnormal CBC presentation.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Establishes pediatric applicability, baseline risk, chronicity, and whether a local specialty protocol should override this general workup.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Known hemoglobinopathy, marrow failure, oncology, transplant, chronic transfusion, or abnormal baseline changes thresholds, consultation, and discharge safety.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for baseline pediatric hematology context; it changes applicability, chronicity, and specialty routing.

source.source_section: history and causes of anaemia

requiredQuestions.label: Ask anemia symptoms and instability history
requiredQuestions.text: Are fatigue, pallor, dizziness, syncope, exertional dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, poor feeding, lethargy, exercise intolerance, or heart failure symptoms present?
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: fatigue / pallor / dizziness / syncope / exertional dyspnea / chest pain / palpitations / poor feeding / lethargy / exercise intolerance / none endorsed / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask whether symptoms are acute, progressive, exertional, or present at rest.
Ask about syncope, chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, poor feeding, or lethargy.
Ask caregivers whether pallor or fatigue is new compared with baseline.
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask whenever pallor, fatigue, abnormal hemoglobin, suspected bleeding, or transfusion need is possible.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Localizes anemia severity and cardiopulmonary strain rather than treating hemoglobin as the only severity marker.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Syncope, chest pain, dyspnea, poor feeding, lethargy, or heart failure symptoms change monitoring, admission, transfusion discussion, and urgent consultation.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for anemia symptom burden; source guidance uses symptoms with Hb, chronicity, and cause to change management.

source.source_section: RBC transfusion indications and anemia symptoms

requiredQuestions.label: Ask bleeding source and severity history
requiredQuestions.text: Is there epistaxis, gum bleeding, oral bleeding, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, hematuria, heavy menstrual bleeding, prolonged bleeding after procedures, or recurrent bleeding episodes?
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: epistaxis / gum bleeding / oral bleeding / hematemesis / melena / hematochezia / hematuria / heavy menstrual bleeding / prolonged procedure bleeding / recurrent episodes / none endorsed / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask current and recent bleeding sites, frequency, duration, and estimated amount.
Ask about GI bleeding, urinary bleeding, menstrual bleeding, and procedure-related bleeding.
Ask whether bleeding is active now or associated with dizziness, syncope, dyspnea, or weakness.
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask whenever bruising, pallor, anemia, thrombocytopenia, petechiae, purpura, or bleeding concern is present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Separates mucocutaneous bleeding, GI/GU blood loss, menstrual blood loss, and procedural bleeding patterns that require different tests and actions.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Active, recurrent, GI/GU, menstrual, or procedure-related bleeding changes coagulation testing, type and screen, transfusion threshold, specialty consultation, and source-specific workup.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for bleeding-source history; management changes by site, severity, hemodynamics, platelet count, and coagulation results.

source.source_section: clinical features and bleeding assessment

requiredQuestions.label: Ask bruising petechiae and trauma pattern history
requiredQuestions.text: Are bruises, petechiae, purpura, non-blanching spots, rapidly spreading lesions, mucosal bleeding, pain, fever, trauma, inconsistent injury story, or mechanical causes such as coughing or vomiting present?
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: easy bruising / petechiae / purpura / non-blanching spots / rapidly spreading lesions / mucosal bleeding / fever / trauma / inconsistent story / mechanical coughing or vomiting / none endorsed / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask when bruising or petechiae started and whether they are spreading.
Ask about fever, mucosal bleeding, pain, trauma, injury explanation, and mechanical causes.
Ask whether petechiae are above the nipple line after coughing or vomiting or more generalized.
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask whenever bruising, petechiae, purpura, thrombocytopenia, non-blanching rash, or safeguarding concern is possible.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Separates mechanical or traumatic petechiae from ITP, sepsis, leukemia, vasculitis, coagulopathy, and non-accidental injury patterns.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Fever, unwell appearance, mucosal bleeding, rapid spread, unexplained bruising, or inconsistent story changes sepsis, hematology, safeguarding, and admission actions.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for bruising and petechiae patterns; guideline-defined danger features and distribution change escalation.

source.source_section: assessment and management of petechiae and purpura

requiredQuestions.label: Ask iron nutrition and blood loss risk history
requiredQuestions.text: Is there excess cow's milk intake, low iron diet, vegetarian or vegan diet, poor intake, pica, prematurity, rapid growth, gastrointestinal symptoms, chronic inflammation, menstrual blood loss, or poor response to prior iron?
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: excess cow's milk / low iron diet / vegetarian or vegan diet / poor intake / pica / prematurity / rapid growth / GI symptoms / chronic inflammation / menstrual blood loss / prior iron nonresponse / none endorsed / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask milk volume, iron-rich foods, dietary restrictions, pica, and supplement adherence.
Ask about prematurity, rapid growth, chronic inflammation, GI symptoms, or blood loss.
Ask whether prior iron was taken consistently and whether hemoglobin or reticulocytes improved.
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when pallor, microcytosis, low ferritin, suspected iron deficiency, fatigue, or chronic anemia is present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies common pediatric iron-deficiency causes and situations where additional workup is needed.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive risk changes ferritin testing, oral iron and diet plan, dosing, adherence counseling, repeat labs, and referral when response is poor or diagnosis is uncertain.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for iron-risk history; ferritin, CBC indices, inflammation context, and response to iron change management.

source.source_section: risk factors, investigations, and treatment

requiredQuestions.label: Ask hemolysis jaundice and dark urine history
requiredQuestions.text: Are jaundice, scleral icterus, dark urine, episodic pallor, abdominal pain, recent infection, medication exposure, family anemia, gallstones, splenectomy, or ethnic/family hemoglobinopathy risk present?
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: jaundice / dark urine / episodic pallor / abdominal pain / recent infection / medication exposure / family anemia / gallstones / splenectomy / hemoglobinopathy risk / none endorsed / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask about jaundice, dark urine, episodic symptoms, abdominal pain, and triggers.
Ask about recent infection, oxidant medications, family anemia, gallstones, splenectomy, and ethnicity-linked hemoglobinopathy risk.
Ask whether symptoms are acute or recurrent.
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when anemia is normocytic or macrocytic, reticulocyte count is high, bilirubin is elevated, urine is dark, jaundice is visible, or hemolysis is possible.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Routes anemia toward hemolysis, hemoglobinopathy, enzymopathy, immune hemolysis, or hepatobiliary mimics.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive hemolysis features change bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin/DAT testing, urine testing, transfusion caution, hematology consultation, and admission threshold.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for hemolysis history; lab-confirmed hemolysis and clinical severity change management.

source.source_section: causes and investigations of anaemia

requiredQuestions.label: Ask leukemia lymphoma and marrow red flags
requiredQuestions.text: Are unexplained petechiae, bruising, pallor, persistent fatigue, fever, recurrent infections, weight loss, night sweats, pruritus, bone pain, limp, lymph node swelling, abdominal fullness, or parental concern present?
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: unexplained petechiae / unexplained bruising / pallor / persistent fatigue / fever / recurrent infections / weight loss / night sweats / pruritus / bone pain or limp / lymph node swelling / abdominal fullness / parental concern / none endorsed / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask about persistent fatigue, fever, recurrent infections, pallor, bruising, petechiae, and bone pain.
Ask about lymph node swelling, abdominal fullness, night sweats, pruritus, weight loss, and parental concern.
Ask whether symptoms are unexplained, persistent, worsening, or grouped together.
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask whenever pediatric anemia, pallor, bruising, petechiae, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, bone pain, or abnormal CBC is present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies NICE child and young-person leukemia and lymphoma referral triggers.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive unexplained features change CBC urgency, blood film review, immediate or very urgent specialist assessment, imaging, admission, and discharge constraints.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for leukemia/lymphoma red flags; NICE referral thresholds guide urgency.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs in children and young people

requiredQuestions.label: Ask medication anticoagulant and family bleeding history
requiredQuestions.text: Has the child used anticoagulants, antiplatelets, NSAIDs, steroids, chemotherapy, antiepileptics, antibiotics, herbal medicines, toxins, or supplements, and is there family history of heavy bleeding, thrombosis, anemia, early gallstones, or consanguinity?
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: anticoagulant / antiplatelet / NSAID / steroid / chemotherapy / antiepileptic / antibiotic / herbal or supplement / toxin exposure / family bleeding / family anemia / early gallstones / consanguinity / none known / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask all prescribed, over-the-counter, herbal, and recent medicines.
Ask about anticoagulant, antiplatelet, NSAID, chemotherapy, antibiotic, antiepileptic, toxin, or supplement exposure.
Ask family history of heavy bleeding, anemia, gallstones, thrombosis, or consanguinity.
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for every pediatric bleeding, bruising, anemia, thrombocytopenia, or abnormal CBC workup.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies medication-induced bleeding or cytopenia, inherited bleeding disorders, inherited anemia, and hemolysis risk.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive exposure or family history changes medication holds, coagulation testing, hemolysis testing, hematology consultation, and counseling.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for medication and family history; source-guided interpretation depends on CBC, film, coagulation tests, and clinical context.

source.source_section: history and causes of anaemia

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask adolescent confidential bleeding and pregnancy context
conditionalQuestions.text: For an adolescent, are heavy or prolonged periods, intermenstrual bleeding, possible pregnancy, postpartum state, sexual activity, eating disorder concern, self-harm, substance use, or confidential safety concerns present?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: heavy periods / prolonged periods / intermenstrual bleeding / possible pregnancy / postpartum / sexual activity / eating disorder concern / self-harm concern / substance use / safety concern / all denied / unable to ask privately
detail_prompts: Ask privately about menstrual bleeding duration, pad/tampon burden, flooding, clots, and anemia symptoms.
Ask pregnancy possibility and postpartum state when clinically applicable.
Ask confidentially about self-harm, substance use, eating disorder concern, and safety.
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask privately when the patient is an adolescent, postmenarchal, pregnancy-capable, has heavy menstrual bleeding, unexplained anemia, or safety concerns.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Separates menstrual blood loss, pregnancy/postpartum complications, nutritional restriction, self-harm, and safety contexts.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive findings change pregnancy testing, gynecology consultation, iron therapy, bleeding treatment, safeguarding, and confidential follow-up.
conditionalQuestions.trigger: adolescent teen heavy period menstruation menstrual pregnancy postpartum self harm eating disorder
conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for adolescent confidential context; it changes safety, pregnancy, gynecology, and bleeding management.

source.source_section: history and causes of anaemia

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask ITP exclusion and low-risk context
conditionalQuestions.text: If ITP is suspected, is the child otherwise well, with no fever, bone pain, weight loss, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, abnormal film, anemia, neutropenia, major bleeding, medication trigger, or safeguarding concern?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: otherwise well / fever / bone pain / weight loss / lymphadenopathy / hepatosplenomegaly / abnormal film / anemia / neutropenia / major bleeding / medication trigger / safeguarding concern / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask whether the child is well between bruising or petechiae episodes.
Ask for ITP exclusions: systemic symptoms, abnormal film, additional cytopenias, organomegaly, lymph nodes, medication triggers, and major bleeding.
Ask whether any safeguarding concern or inconsistent injury history exists.
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when thrombocytopenia, petechiae, purpura, or bruising suggests possible ITP.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Prevents an ITP label from being applied to children with alternative-diagnosis features.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Any exclusion feature changes from observation-focused ITP care toward urgent senior review, hematology consultation, additional labs, admission, or safeguarding route.
conditionalQuestions.trigger: itp thrombocytopenia low platelets platelet count isolated thrombocytopenia
conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for ITP exclusions; RCH defines ITP by isolated thrombocytopenia in a well child with otherwise normal FBE and film.

source.source_section: assessment and differential diagnosis

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask transfusion and specialty protocol context
conditionalQuestions.text: Is there chronic transfusion, thalassemia, sickle cell disease, congenital anemia, congenital heart disease, chemotherapy, transplant, irradiated-product need, prior transfusion reaction, or family refusal or consent barrier?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: chronic transfusion / thalassemia / sickle cell disease / congenital anemia / congenital heart disease / chemotherapy / transplant / irradiated product need / prior transfusion reaction / consent barrier / none known / unable to assess
detail_prompts: Ask about chronic transfusion program, hemoglobinopathy, congenital anemia, cardiac disease, chemotherapy, transplant, or irradiated-product need.
Ask about prior transfusion reaction, blood-product restrictions, consent barriers, and local hematology plan.
Ask whether a specialty protocol already defines thresholds or product choice.
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when transfusion, severe anemia, chronic anemia, hemoglobinopathy, oncology, transplant, or blood-product prescription is possible.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies situations where general RBC thresholds are modified by disease, product requirements, or local hematology protocol.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes transfusion threshold, product selection, consent, hematology consultation, and whether this module should defer to local specialty guidance.
conditionalQuestions.trigger: transfusion hemoglobinopathy thalassemia sickle congenital heart chemotherapy transplant irradiated product
conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for transfusion protocol context; it changes management thresholds and product safety.

source.source_section: blood product prescription and special patient groups

requiredExam.label: Inspect conjunctival pallor
requiredExam.technique: Use ambient light or a penlight to inspect lower palpebral conjunctiva while avoiding pressure on the eye; compare with mucosa and clinical context.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when pallor, anemia, fatigue, bleeding, hemolysis, or abnormal CBC is being evaluated.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Visible anemia clue, jaundice clue, and need to correlate with hemoglobin and hemolysis testing.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated pediatric heme sources; interpret pallor with CBC and clinical severity.
requiredExam.management_change: Marked pallor or jaundice changes urgency of CBC, hemolysis testing, transfusion discussion, admission, and specialty consultation.
requiredExam.limitations: Lighting, baseline skin tone, crying, and examiner variability limit accuracy; a normal look does not exclude anemia.

source.source_section: clinical features and investigations

requiredExam.label: Inspect petechiae distribution
requiredExam.technique: Inspect visible skin for petechiae, purpura, bruises, lesion size, distribution above or below the nipple line, dependent areas, pressure areas, and whether lesions blanch with gentle pressure.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when bruising, petechiae, purpura, thrombocytopenia, fever, bleeding, or non-blanching rash is possible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Mechanical petechiae, ITP, sepsis, meningococcal disease, leukemia, vasculitis, coagulopathy, trauma, or safeguarding concern.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; distribution and unwell appearance change guideline-defined escalation.
requiredExam.management_change: Generalized, non-blanching, purpuric, rapidly spreading, or unexplained lesions change sepsis, CBC/film, hematology, observation, admission, and safeguarding decisions.
requiredExam.equipment_needed: good lighting and clear tumbler or gentle pressure
requiredExam.limitations: A partial skin view can miss dependent or covered lesions; privacy and safeguarding sensitivity are important.

source.source_section: definitions and assessment

requiredExam.label: Inspect oral mucosal bleeding
requiredExam.technique: Inspect lips, gums, buccal mucosa, palate, and tongue for petechiae, wet purpura, gum bleeding, ulcers, pallor, or trauma using a light when needed.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when thrombocytopenia, ITP, bleeding, bruising, petechiae, anticoagulant exposure, or coagulopathy concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Clinically important mucosal bleeding, ITP severity, coagulopathy, trauma, infection, or leukemia/chemotherapy mucosal disease.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; mucosal bleeding changes treatment and observation decisions in source guidance.
requiredExam.management_change: Wet purpura or active mucosal bleeding changes hematology consultation, treatment consideration, admission, platelet/bleeding support, and activity restrictions.
requiredExam.limitations: Young children may not tolerate oral inspection; avoid provoking bleeding.

source.source_section: clinical features and management

requiredExam.label: Palpate cervical lymph nodes
requiredExam.technique: Palpate anterior and posterior cervical chains gently and document size, tenderness, mobility, firmness, fixation, and whether nodes are localized or generalized by history and targeted exam.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when pallor, fatigue, bruising, petechiae, fever, infection recurrence, weight loss, night sweats, pruritus, or lymphoma/leukemia concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Reactive lymphadenopathy, leukemia, lymphoma, infection, inflammatory disease, or malignancy referral trigger.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; NICE referral guidance uses unexplained lymphadenopathy and associated symptoms to guide urgency.
requiredExam.management_change: Unexplained, generalized, firm, fixed, supraclavicular, or systemic-symptom-associated nodes change CBC urgency, imaging/referral consideration, and discharge safety.
requiredExam.limitations: Small reactive nodes are common; interpret with duration, distribution, fever, weight loss, night sweats, pruritus, and CBC/film.

source.source_section: lymphadenopathy and associated symptoms in children and young people

requiredExam.label: Palpate liver edge
requiredExam.technique: With the child supine and relaxed, palpate from the right lower abdomen toward the costal margin during inspiration and document liver edge distance, tenderness, and consistency.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when anemia, hemolysis, leukemia/lymphoma concern, abdominal fullness, jaundice, or systemic symptoms are present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Hepatomegaly from hemolysis, infection, leukemia, lymphoma, liver disease, heart failure, or storage/infiltrative disease.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; hepatomegaly changes red-flag and referral routing when paired with heme symptoms.
requiredExam.management_change: Hepatomegaly changes CBC/film, hemolysis/liver testing, imaging consideration, hematology/oncology referral, admission, and transfusion safety.
requiredExam.limitations: Body habitus, crying, distension, and examiner variability affect palpation; ultrasound may be needed when exam is uncertain and management depends on size.

source.source_section: splenomegaly and associated symptoms in children and young people

requiredExam.label: Palpate spleen tip
requiredExam.technique: With the child supine or right lateral if needed, palpate from the right lower abdomen toward the left costal margin during inspiration and document whether a spleen tip or enlargement is felt.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when anemia, hemolysis, recurrent infection, lymphadenopathy, weight loss, night sweats, pruritus, abdominal fullness, or malignancy concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Splenomegaly from hemolysis, leukemia, lymphoma, infection, portal hypertension, storage disease, or hemoglobinopathy.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; splenomegaly with symptoms is a source-defined referral and escalation cue.
requiredExam.management_change: Splenomegaly changes CBC/film urgency, hemolysis workup, lymphoma/leukemia referral, imaging consideration, activity precautions, admission, and transfusion planning.
requiredExam.limitations: A nonpalpable spleen does not exclude pathology; body habitus and cooperation limit sensitivity.

source.source_section: splenomegaly and associated symptoms in children and young people

conditionalExam.label: Inspect anterior nares
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect the anterior nares with light for active bleeding, clot, crusting, trauma, septal lesion, or unilateral source when epistaxis is reported.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when epistaxis, recurrent nosebleeds, anticoagulant exposure, thrombocytopenia, or active bleeding is reported.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Epistaxis source, local trauma, mucosal bleeding, thrombocytopenia severity, or coagulopathy.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for anterior nares inspection; active bleeding changes immediate management.
conditionalExam.management_change: Active bleeding changes local pressure/topical therapy, labs, transfusion or hemostasis support, ENT/hematology consultation, and observation.
conditionalExam.limitations: Posterior bleeding can be missed; do not delay resuscitation for heavy bleeding.

source.source_section: bleeding features and management

conditionalExam.label: Palpate axillary lymph nodes
conditionalExam.technique: Palpate each axilla with the child's arm relaxed and document node size, tenderness, mobility, firmness, fixation, and symmetry.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when cervical nodes are abnormal, generalized lymphadenopathy is suspected, or systemic leukemia/lymphoma features are present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Generalized lymphadenopathy, lymphoma, leukemia, infection, or inflammatory disease.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; generalized lymphadenopathy and systemic features change referral urgency.
conditionalExam.management_change: Axillary lymphadenopathy with systemic features changes CBC/film, imaging, specialist referral, and discharge safety.
conditionalExam.limitations: Reactive nodes are common; interpret with distribution, duration, symptoms, and CBC/film.
conditionalExam.trigger: lymphadenopathy generalized nodes lymphoma leukemia night sweats weight loss pruritus

source.source_section: lymphadenopathy in children and young people

conditionalExam.label: Palpate inguinal lymph nodes
conditionalExam.technique: Palpate inguinal regions when clinically appropriate and document node size, tenderness, mobility, firmness, fixation, and whether findings are localized or generalized.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when generalized lymphadenopathy, lower-extremity infection, lymphoma/leukemia concern, or systemic symptoms are present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Generalized lymphadenopathy, local infection, lymphoma, leukemia, or inflammatory disease.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; generalized lymphadenopathy and systemic features change referral urgency.
conditionalExam.management_change: Inguinal lymphadenopathy with generalized or systemic findings changes CBC/film, referral, imaging consideration, and discharge safety.
conditionalExam.limitations: Respect privacy and chaperone needs; local infection and reactive nodes are common.
conditionalExam.trigger: lymphadenopathy generalized nodes lymphoma leukemia leg infection systemic

source.source_section: lymphadenopathy in children and young people

conditionalExam.label: Inspect target joint swelling
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect the symptomatic joint for swelling, warmth, bruising, deformity, range-limiting pain, and visible hemarthrosis pattern before palpation or movement.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when joint pain, swelling, hemarthrosis concern, trauma, bleeding disorder, anticoagulant exposure, leukemia, or bone pain is reported.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Hemarthrosis, trauma, septic arthritis, leukemia-related bone pain, inflammatory arthritis, or safeguarding concern.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; joint findings change bleeding-disorder, infection, trauma, and malignancy routing.
conditionalExam.management_change: Joint swelling or severe pain changes coagulation testing, imaging, orthopedic/infection route, hematology consultation, analgesia, and activity precautions.
conditionalExam.limitations: This is not a full MSK workup; use pediatric MSK pathway if limp, hot joint, non-weight-bearing, or trauma dominates.
conditionalExam.trigger: joint swelling hemarthrosis bone pain limp trauma bleeding disorder anticoagulant

source.source_section: bone pain and leukemia in children and young people

redFlags.label: Active bleeding with shock or altered state
redFlags.action: Escalate immediately for active heavy bleeding, suspected internal bleeding, hypotension, tachycardia with poor perfusion, syncope, chest pain, dyspnea, or altered mental status.
redFlags.rationale: Hemodynamic compromise or altered state means the child needs emergency stabilization, IV access, labs, blood-product planning, and senior help rather than routine outpatient anemia workup.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Major hemorrhage, severe anemia, coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, trauma, sepsis, or intracranial bleeding.
redFlags.management_change: Triggers emergency response, IV access, type and screen or crossmatch, transfusion discussion, resuscitation, urgent source control, admission, and specialty consultation.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this emergency red flag; instability changes management immediately.
redFlags.limitations: Do not wait for complete lab workup before resuscitation when the child is unstable.

source.source_section: RBC transfusion indications and clinical setting

redFlags.label: Petechiae or purpura with fever or unwell appearance
redFlags.action: Treat fever or unwell appearance with petechiae or purpura as a senior-review and possible sepsis or meningococcal presentation until serious bacterial infection is excluded.
redFlags.rationale: RCH petechiae/purpura guidance requires careful assessment for serious bacterial infection, especially when the child is unwell or febrile.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Meningococcal disease, serious bacterial infection, sepsis, ITP, leukemia, vasculitis, or mechanical petechiae.
redFlags.management_change: Changes monitoring, cultures/labs, antibiotics, sepsis pathway, admission or observation, and discharge safety.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this red flag; guideline-defined danger features change escalation.
redFlags.limitations: Most petechiae are not meningococcal disease, but reassurance requires structured assessment and safety netting.

source.source_section: key points and management

redFlags.label: Unexplained petechiae bruising pallor fatigue fever infection or bone pain
redFlags.action: Escalate for immediate or very urgent CBC and specialist assessment when unexplained petechiae, bruising, pallor, persistent fatigue, unexplained fever, recurrent infection, or persistent bone pain is present.
redFlags.rationale: NICE NG12 lists these findings as leukemia referral triggers in children and young people.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Leukemia, marrow failure, lymphoma, infection, inflammatory disease, or bone malignancy mimic.
redFlags.management_change: Changes CBC urgency, film review, hematology/oncology referral, discharge constraints, and follow-up ownership.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this referral red flag; NICE uses symptom-triggered urgent CBC and specialist assessment thresholds.
redFlags.limitations: Symptoms are nonspecific; combinations, persistence, unexplained nature, and abnormal CBC increase urgency.

source.source_section: leukemia symptoms and signs in children and young people

redFlags.label: Lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly with systemic features
redFlags.action: Consider very urgent specialist referral when unexplained lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly is associated with fever, night sweats, shortness of breath, pruritus, or weight loss.
redFlags.rationale: NICE NG12 identifies these child and young-person combinations as lymphoma referral triggers.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, leukemia, infection, or inflammatory disease.
redFlags.management_change: Changes CBC/film urgency, imaging/referral route, admission safety, and discharge follow-up.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this referral red flag; NICE uses associated symptoms and referral timing rather than LR+/LR-.
redFlags.limitations: Localized tender nodes often reflect infection, but unexplained or generalized lymphadenopathy with systemic features needs structured escalation.

source.source_section: lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly referral triggers

redFlags.label: ITP concern with abnormal film cytopenias systemic features or major bleeding
redFlags.action: Do not treat as low-risk ITP when the child is unwell or has fever, bone pain, weight loss, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, abnormal blood film, anemia, neutropenia, major bleeding, or safeguarding concern.
redFlags.rationale: RCH ITP requires isolated thrombocytopenia in a well child with otherwise normal FBE and film; alternative causes must be excluded.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Leukemia, marrow failure, sepsis, DIC, medication-induced cytopenia, trauma, safeguarding concern, or severe bleeding disorder.
redFlags.management_change: Changes from observation or outpatient ITP advice to urgent senior review, hematology consultation, additional tests, admission, or safeguarding route.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this ITP exclusion rule; source-defined exclusion features change management.
redFlags.limitations: ITP has no single confirmatory lab test; do not anchor on platelet count alone.

source.source_section: background, assessment, and differential diagnosis

redFlags.label: Severe anemia or symptomatic Hb below transfusion range
redFlags.action: Escalate when Hb is below 70 g/L, or Hb is 70 to 90 g/L with bleeding, hemolysis, clinical anemia symptoms, cardiac disease, or other high-risk setting.
redFlags.rationale: RCH blood product guidance says RBC transfusion is often indicated below 70 g/L and may be indicated at 70 to 90 g/L depending on clinical setting and symptoms.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Severe anemia, acute blood loss, hemolysis, symptomatic chronic anemia, or transfusion need.
redFlags.management_change: Changes admission, IV access, type and screen, transfusion discussion, monitoring, and specialist consultation.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to hemoglobin transfusion thresholds; management follows source thresholds, symptoms, cause, and local policy.
redFlags.limitations: Children with chronic anemia, hemoglobinopathy, cyanotic heart disease, or active bleeding may need individualized thresholds.

source.source_section: RBC transfusion indications

initialTests.label: Full blood examination film and reticulocyte count
initialTests.action: Obtain CBC or FBE with differential, platelet count, reticulocyte count, and blood film review for anemia, bruising, petechiae, thrombocytopenia, systemic features, or malignancy concern.
initialTests.rationale: RCH anemia and ITP guidance rely on FBE/CBC, film, and reticulocyte pattern to separate anemia causes, isolated ITP, cytopenias, and red flags.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Hemoglobin severity, platelet count, neutropenia, pancytopenia, reticulocyte response, abnormal blasts or film features, ITP eligibility, hemolysis, marrow failure, or leukemia.
initialTests.management_change: Abnormal hemoglobin, platelets, neutrophils, reticulocytes, or film changes urgency, hematology consultation, transfusion, admission, and referral.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not encoded for this test bundle; individual results and film review drive source-backed management.
initialTests.limitations: Repeat or urgent hematology review may be needed if the clinical picture changes or film is abnormal.
reference_thresholds: ITP frame requires platelet count <100 x 10^9/L with otherwise normal FBE and film in a well child.
Hb <70 g/L often indicates RBC transfusion; Hb 70-90 g/L may indicate transfusion depending on symptoms and setting.

source.source_section: investigations

initialTests.label: Ferritin and iron deficiency assessment
initialTests.action: Check ferritin when iron deficiency is suspected and interpret with inflammation context; use CBC indices and response to therapy rather than broad iron panels alone.
initialTests.rationale: RCH iron deficiency guidance states ferritin is the most useful screening test for iron stores and that reduced ferritin below 20 microgram/L indicates borderline or low stores.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Iron deficiency with or without anemia, inflammation-masked ferritin, nutritional deficiency, chronic blood loss, or poor response to iron.
initialTests.management_change: Low ferritin changes oral iron and dietary plan, dosing, adherence counseling, repeat labs, and escalation if severe or nonresponsive.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not encoded for ferritin here; source thresholds and inflammation context guide treatment.
initialTests.limitations: Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and may be falsely normal or high with inflammation.
reference_thresholds: Ferritin <20 microgram/L indicates borderline or low iron stores in RCH guidance.
Severe iron deficiency anemia is Hb 80 g/L or less for RCH higher-dose oral iron framing.

source.source_section: assessment and investigations

initialTests.label: Coagulation and fibrinogen tests
initialTests.action: Order PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, and clinically indicated coagulation studies when bleeding is significant, unexplained, recurrent, procedure-related, trauma-discordant, or not explained by isolated thrombocytopenia.
initialTests.rationale: Bleeding pattern and platelet/CBC context should guide coagulopathy testing rather than assuming all bruising is ITP or iron deficiency.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Coagulopathy, DIC, liver disease, inherited bleeding disorder, medication effect, or thrombocytopenia-associated bleeding.
initialTests.management_change: Abnormal results change bleeding treatment, factor or plasma discussion, medication holds, hematology consultation, procedure safety, and admission.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not encoded for this broad coagulation screen; abnormal tests require source and hematology interpretation.
initialTests.limitations: Screening tests can be normal in some platelet function or von Willebrand disorders; targeted tests may need hematology input.

source.source_section: investigations and alternative causes

initialTests.label: Group screen and crossmatch when transfusion possible
initialTests.action: Obtain group and screen or crossmatch when Hb, symptoms, active bleeding, hemolysis, or clinical setting makes transfusion possible.
initialTests.rationale: RCH transfusion thresholds depend on Hb and clinical setting, so transfusion planning should start before deterioration when severe anemia or active bleeding is plausible.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Transfusion readiness, severe anemia, active bleeding, hemolysis, or blood-product safety.
initialTests.management_change: Positive threshold or active bleeding changes IV access, consent, product ordering, monitoring, admission, and consultation.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to group and screen; it is a safety and readiness action when transfusion may change management.
initialTests.limitations: Do not delay emergency transfusion for administrative completion when local massive hemorrhage or emergency-release protocol applies.

source.source_section: blood product prescription and RBC transfusion indications

initialTests.label: Hemolysis bilirubin LDH haptoglobin DAT and urine tests
initialTests.action: Order bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin, direct antiglobulin test, urinalysis for blood or hemoglobin, and renal function when hemolysis, jaundice, dark urine, high reticulocytes, or acute anemia is suspected.
initialTests.rationale: RCH anemia guidance selects additional investigations by clinical features; jaundice, dark urine, and reticulocyte pattern shift the workup toward hemolysis.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Immune hemolysis, hemoglobinopathy, enzymopathy, microangiopathy, hemoglobinuria, renal injury, or hepatobiliary mimic.
initialTests.management_change: Positive hemolysis tests change admission, transfusion caution, hematology consultation, medication review, renal monitoring, and cause-specific therapy.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not encoded for this hemolysis test cluster; result pattern and severity change management.
initialTests.limitations: Haptoglobin and LDH interpretation varies by age, inflammation, sample hemolysis, and liver disease.

source.source_section: investigations selected by clinical features

initialTests.label: B12 folate renal liver and inflammatory tests when indicated
initialTests.action: Add B12, folate, UEC, LFT, ESR or CRP, and other targeted tests when macrocytosis, poor feeding, vegan or vegetarian diet, neurologic features, jaundice, chronic disease, organomegaly, or systemic symptoms are present.
initialTests.rationale: RCH anemia guidance notes B12 deficiency risks and recommends further investigation such as UEC, LFT, and ESR depending on clinical features.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: B12 or folate deficiency, renal disease, liver disease, chronic inflammation, marrow disease, infection, or systemic illness.
initialTests.management_change: Abnormal targeted tests change replacement therapy, specialty referral, admission, and follow-up.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not encoded for this targeted test set; use only when source-defined clinical features make results management-changing.
initialTests.limitations: Do not order broad panels without a clinical trigger; interpret age-specific ranges and local lab units.

source.source_section: B12 deficiency and further investigations

initialTests.label: Pregnancy test when adolescent bleeding or anemia context applies
initialTests.action: Obtain pregnancy testing for pregnancy-capable adolescents when heavy bleeding, abdominal pain, syncope, anemia, possible pregnancy, postpartum state, or confidential history makes pregnancy-related bleeding possible.
initialTests.rationale: Pregnancy status materially changes adolescent bleeding and anemia management, transfusion risk, imaging, medication, and consultation.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Pregnancy-related bleeding, ectopic pregnancy mimic, postpartum bleeding context, or pregnancy-associated anemia.
initialTests.management_change: Positive pregnancy test changes obstetric/gynecology route, medication safety, imaging, transfusion planning, and emergency escalation.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to pregnancy testing as a binary management gate in adolescent bleeding or anemia context.
initialTests.limitations: Ask privately and use local consent/confidentiality rules; do not expose pregnancy testing unnecessarily in prepubertal children.

source.source_section: history and causes of anaemia

dispositionRules.label: Emergency stabilization route
dispositionRules.action: Use local emergency, transfusion, hematology, critical care, sepsis, trauma, or safeguarding pathway when the child is unstable, actively bleeding heavily, altered, febrile with petechiae, or has suspected intracranial or internal bleeding.
dispositionRules.rationale: Unstable bleeding or serious petechiae presentations require immediate action before routine outpatient heme classification.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Major bleeding, severe anemia, sepsis, meningococcal disease, trauma, safeguarding concern, or intracranial bleeding.
dispositionRules.management_change: Triggers resuscitation, senior review, IV access, urgent labs, blood-product readiness, empiric therapy when indicated, admission, and specialty handoff.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to emergency disposition; source-defined instability and danger features change management immediately.
dispositionRules.limitations: This module supports documentation and routing but does not replace local resuscitation or transfusion protocols.

source.source_section: management

dispositionRules.label: RBC transfusion threshold route
dispositionRules.action: Discuss RBC transfusion when Hb is below 70 g/L, or Hb is 70 to 90 g/L with bleeding, hemolysis, symptomatic anemia, cardiac disease, or another high-risk setting; avoid routine transfusion above 90 g/L unless a specific situation warrants it.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH blood product guidance gives pediatric Hb ranges and emphasizes clinical features and setting.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Transfusion need in pediatric severe anemia, acute bleeding, hemolysis, chronic anemia, cardiac disease, or hemoglobinopathy.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes group and screen, consent, product ordering, monitoring, admission, and hematology or critical-care consultation.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to transfusion threshold management; use Hb, symptoms, cause, and local policy.
dispositionRules.limitations: Thresholds are individualized for chronic anemia, hemoglobinopathies, congenital heart disease, active bleeding, oncology, and local specialty protocols.
reference_thresholds: Hb <70 g/L: RBC transfusion is often indicated.
Hb 70-90 g/L: RBC transfusion may be indicated depending on clinical setting and symptoms.
Hb >90 g/L: RBC transfusion is often unnecessary and may be inappropriate.

source.source_section: indications for red blood cell transfusion

dispositionRules.label: Iron therapy and diet route
dispositionRules.action: For likely iron deficiency with stable child and no red flags, start oral iron plus dietary change, document weight-based dosing plan, address adherence, and arrange repeat hemoglobin and reticulocyte or ferritin follow-up per local practice.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH iron deficiency guidance states most children with iron deficiency with or without anemia can be treated with oral iron supplementation and dietary modification.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Iron deficiency with or without anemia and safe outpatient treatment eligibility.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes medication plan, dietary counseling, dosing by weight, adherence strategy, follow-up labs, and referral when severe or nonresponsive.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to iron-treatment action; ferritin, CBC pattern, severity, and response guide management.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not use oral iron alone for unstable severe anemia, active bleeding, suspected malignancy, hemolysis, or unclear diagnosis with red flags.
reference_thresholds: Ferritin <20 microgram/L indicates borderline or low iron stores.
Severe IDA Hb 80 g/L or less uses higher-dose oral iron framing in RCH guidance.

source.source_section: treatment and dosing

dispositionRules.label: ITP observation treatment and precautions route
dispositionRules.action: For suspected ITP, base treatment on bleeding features and exclusion of alternative causes; provide activity, medication, head-injury, and return-precaution advice, and consult hematology for significant bleeding or atypical features.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH ITP guidance states treatment decisions are based on clinical features, not platelet count alone, and most children do not require treatment.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Low-risk ITP, clinically significant ITP bleeding, atypical thrombocytopenia, leukemia mimic, or coagulopathy.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes observation, hematology discussion, treatment consideration, admission, activity restriction, medication avoidance, and return precautions.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to ITP management disposition; bleeding features and exclusion criteria change treatment.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not label as low-risk ITP if the child is unwell or has abnormal film, additional cytopenias, organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, major bleeding, or safeguarding concern.
reference_thresholds: ITP is isolated platelet count <100 x 10^9/L in a well child with otherwise normal FBE and film.
Intracranial hemorrhage risk is very low, under 1%, but head injury or neurologic symptoms require urgent escalation.

source.source_section: key points and management

dispositionRules.label: Leukemia lymphoma urgent referral route
dispositionRules.action: Use immediate specialist assessment for unexplained petechiae, very urgent CBC for unexplained bruising, pallor, persistent fatigue, fever, infection, or bone pain, and very urgent specialist referral for unexplained lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly with systemic features.
dispositionRules.rationale: NICE NG12 defines child and young-person referral actions for leukemia and lymphoma symptoms.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Leukemia, lymphoma, marrow failure, bone malignancy mimic, or serious systemic disease.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes CBC timing, specialist ownership, imaging/referral route, admission or discharge constraints, and safety-netting.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to NICE referral urgency; source-defined symptoms and signs determine action.
dispositionRules.limitations: NICE guidance is a referral framework; local emergency, oncology, and hematology pathways may be more urgent when the child is unstable or labs are abnormal.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs in children and young people

dispositionRules.label: Hematology handoff and review owner route
dispositionRules.action: Hand off concise non-PHI traceable details: symptoms, bleeding sites, vitals, mental status, weight, CBC/differential/reticulocyte/film, ferritin or hemolysis/coagulation tests, red flags, source IDs, and the specific result that changed management.
dispositionRules.rationale: Clinician adoption requires a reviewable audit trail that explains why each result changes management and which guideline row supports it.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Safe hematology review, source-backed follow-up, transfusion planning, and knowledge-pack traceability.
dispositionRules.management_change: Improves consultation quality, prevents unsupported authoritative prose, and supports reviewer audit without exposing PHI.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to handoff structure; this action preserves traceability and auditability.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not include patient identifiers in copied audit text; keep source IDs and clinical facts only.

criteria_options.label: Pediatric anemia is Hb below age cutoff: 2 mo <90 g/L; 2-6 mo <95; 6-24 mo <105; 2-11 y <115; >12 y female <120, male <130
criteria_options.criteria_text: Define anemia by hemoglobin below the lower limit for age: 2 months 90 g/L, 2-6 months 95 g/L, 6-24 months 105 g/L, 2-11 years 115 g/L, and >12 years female 120 g/L or male 130 g/L.

criteria_options.label: Pediatric anemia red-flag admission: Hb <60 g/L or tachycardia/murmur/heart failure, hemolysis, reticulocytopenia, nucleated RBCs, thrombocytopenia/neutropenia
criteria_options.criteria_text: Consider admission and hematology/paediatric consultation when Hb is <60 g/L or red flags are present, including heart failure signs, hemolysis, reticulocytopenia, nucleated red cells, thrombocytopenia, or neutropenia.

criteria_options.label: Microcytosis mimic: beta-thalassemia trait supported by HbA2 >3.5% on electrophoresis/HPLC after iron deficiency addressed
criteria_options.criteria_text: In microcytic anemia, beta-thalassemia trait is diagnosed on HPLC or hemoglobin electrophoresis with HbA2 >3.5%, noting HbA2 may be masked by iron deficiency.

criteria_options.label: Pediatric treatment frame: transfusion threshold, iron replacement, bleeding control, and specialty handoff depend on symptoms, hemoglobin, cause, and chronicity
criteria_options.criteria_text: Pediatric treatment frame: transfusion threshold, iron replacement, bleeding control, and specialty handoff depend on symptoms, hemoglobin, cause, and chronicity Use Hb threshold, symptoms, bleeding/hemolysis, chronic disease, hemoglobinopathy, iron deficiency, and local blood-product procedures to decide transfusion, iron therapy, consultation, and follow-up. Positive thresholds or symptoms change admission, IV access, group and screen, transfusion discussion, iron dose selection, and repeat lab timing. Transfusion need, iron deficiency treatment, specialty handoff, and follow-up safety. RCH blood product guidance often indicates RBC transfusion below 70 g/L, may indicate transfusion at 70 to 90 g/L depending on clinical setting and anemia symptoms, and often avoids transfusion above 90 g/L; RCH iron deficiency guidance treats most children with oral iron and diet change.
criteria_options.source_section: indications for red blood cell transfusion

criteria_options.label: Severe anemia or symptomatic Hb below transfusion range
criteria_options.criteria_text: Severe anemia or symptomatic Hb below transfusion range Escalate when Hb is below 70 g/L, or Hb is 70 to 90 g/L with bleeding, hemolysis, clinical anemia symptoms, cardiac disease, or other high-risk setting. Changes admission, IV access, type and screen, transfusion discussion, monitoring, and specialist consultation. Severe anemia, acute blood loss, hemolysis, symptomatic chronic anemia, or transfusion need. RCH blood product guidance says RBC transfusion is often indicated below 70 g/L and may be indicated at 70 to 90 g/L depending on clinical setting and symptoms.

criteria_options.label: RBC transfusion threshold route
criteria_options.criteria_text: RBC transfusion threshold route Discuss RBC transfusion when Hb is below 70 g/L, or Hb is 70 to 90 g/L with bleeding, hemolysis, symptomatic anemia, cardiac disease, or another high-risk setting; avoid routine transfusion above 90 g/L unless a specific situation warrants it. Changes group and screen, consent, product ordering, monitoring, admission, and hematology or critical-care consultation. Transfusion need in pediatric severe anemia, acute bleeding, hemolysis, chronic anemia, cardiac disease, or hemoglobinopathy. RCH blood product guidance gives pediatric Hb ranges and emphasizes clinical features and setting.
